perturbation.original_text: (i) at any time from and after the date hereof, except to the extent required by law or in connection with any legal proceeding of which the Stockholder has theretofore given written notice to the Buyer, divulge, transmit or otherwise disclose or cause to be divulged, transmitted or otherwise disclosed, any business contacts, client or customer lists, technology, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, contracts or other confidential or proprietary information of the Seller or the Buyer of whatever nature, whether existing on or prior to the date hereof or arising from and after the date hereof (provided, however, that for purposes hereof, information shall not be considered to be confidential or proprietary if (A) it is a matter of common knowledge or public record, (B) it is generally known in the industry, or (C) the Stockholder can demonstrate that such information was already known to the recipient thereof other than by reason of any breach of any obligation under this Agreement or any other confidentiality or non- disclosure agreement known to the Stockholder);
perturbation.changed_text: (i) at any time from and after the date hereof, except to the extent required by law or in connection with any legal proceeding of which the Stockholder has theretofore given written notice to the Buyer, divulge, transmit or otherwise disclose or cause to be divulged, transmitted or otherwise disclosed, any business contacts, client or customer lists, technology, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, contracts or other confidential or proprietary information of the Seller or the Buyer of whatever nature, whether existing on or prior to the date hereof or arising from and after the date hereof (provided, however, that for purposes hereof, information shall not be considered to be confidential or proprietary if (A) it is easily discoverable, (B) it is generally known, or (C) the Stockholder can demonstrate that such information was already known to the recipient thereof);
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of confidential information from 'matter of common knowledge or public record' to 'easily discoverable' weakens trade secret protection. 'Easily discoverable' is a more subjective standard than 'public record', which is legally defined, making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines 'trade secret' as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. Replacing 'matter of common knowledge or public record' with 'easily discoverable' contradicts this definition, as information that is easily discoverable may still be a trade secret under the UTSA if it is not generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means. The modified definition creates ambiguity and weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The change from 'matter of common knowledge or public record' to 'easily discoverable' weakens the protection of trade secrets, as 'easily discoverable' introduces a subjective standard. The Law Explanation states the UTSA defines 'trade secret' as information not 'readily ascertainable by proper means'. The modified definition creates ambiguity, contradicting the intent to protect trade secrets, thereby fulfilling the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction'. Since one scraped law snippet is a 404 error, I cannot use those to further justify my answer.

perturbation.original_text: The Stockholder and the Buyer hereby acknowledge and agree that any breach by the Stockholder, directly or indirectly, of the foregoing restrictive covenants will cause the Buyer and/or its affiliates irreparable injury for which there is no adequate remedy at law. Accordingly, the Stockholder expressly agrees that, in the event of any such breach or any threatened breach hereunder by the Stockholder, directly or indirectly, the Buyer and its subsidiaries shall be entitled, in addition to any and all other remedies available, to seek and obtain injunctive and/or other equitable relief to require specific performance of or prevent, restrain and/or enjoin a breach under the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Stockholder and the Buyer hereby acknowledge and agree that any breach by the Stockholder, directly or indirectly, of the foregoing restrictive covenants may cause the Buyer and/or its affiliates some injury for which there may be an adequate remedy at law. Accordingly, the Stockholder expressly agrees that, in the event of any such breach or any threatened breach hereunder by the Stockholder, directly or indirectly, the Buyer and its subsidiaries shall be entitled, to seek and obtain monetary damages, and only if such are inadequate, may seek injunctive and/or other equitable relief.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'irreparable injury for which there is no adequate remedy at law' to 'some injury for which there may be an adequate remedy at law' and subordinating injunctive relief to monetary damages undermines the ability to obtain an injunction for breach of the non-compete agreement. This contradicts the typical legal standard for granting injunctions in non-compete cases.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 359 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts states that specific performance or an injunction will not be ordered if damages would be adequate to protect the expectation interest of the injured party. However, in non-compete agreements, irreparable harm is often presumed and monetary damages are difficult to calculate, justifying injunctive relief. The modification suggests that monetary damages are the primary remedy and injunctive relief is only available if damages are inadequate, potentially contradicting the approach courts often take in enforcing non-compete clauses, and thus, specific performance (injunctions).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates that a breach of the restrictive covenants would cause irreparable injury without adequate legal remedy, entitling the Buyer to injunctive relief. The changed text alters this, suggesting that monetary damages may be adequate and subordinating injunctive relief. This contradicts the general principle, as supported by the 'Wex' snippet, that 'specific performance is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach'. By prioritizing monetary damages and suggesting they may be adequate, the modified text undermines the ability to obtain an injunction, which is a common remedy in non-compete agreements where damages are hard to quantify and irreparable harm is presumed.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York. The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of Florida and Georgia state and federal courts with respect to any action between the parties relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, excluding its conflict of laws principles. The parties agree to the jurisdiction and venue of any court deemed appropriate by the Buyer with respect to any action between the parties relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specification of Florida and Georgia courts and giving the Buyer the power to choose any court introduces uncertainty and contradicts the principle of agreed-upon jurisdiction. Excluding 'conflict of laws principles' can lead to unexpected choice of law implications.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1391(b) provides rules for venue in federal court. While parties can contractually agree to a forum, a clause that allows one party to choose 'any court deemed appropriate' may be challenged as violating the spirit of fair venue selection, especially if the chosen venue has no connection to the parties or the dispute. The original clause specifying Florida and Georgia courts provides certainty that is absent in the modified version, introducing a potential conflict with the principles underlying venue statutes.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a legal contradiction by giving the Buyer the power to choose 'any court deemed appropriate.' This contradicts the principle of agreed-upon jurisdiction and the spirit of fair venue selection as highlighted in 28 U.S.C. § 1391. The original clause specifying Florida and Georgia courts provides a certainty that is absent in the modified version. The scraped snippets from 28 USC 1391 emphasize the importance of venue in civil actions brought in district courts of the United States, reinforcing the idea that venue should be established based on specific criteria rather than arbitrary choice. The modification undermines this by potentially allowing the Buyer to choose a venue with no connection to the parties or the dispute, which could be challenged as violating the underlying principles of venue statutes.

perturbation.original_text: in the event that any of the foregoing restrictions, or any portion of the foregoing restrictions, shall be deemed contrary to law, invalid or unenforceable in any respect by any court or other tribunal of competent jurisdiction, then such restrictions shall be deemed to be amended, modified and reduced in scope and effect, only to that extent necessary to render same valid and enforceable, and any other of the foregoing restrictions shall be unaffected and shall remain in full force and effect.
perturbation.changed_text: in the event that any of the foregoing restrictions, or any portion of the foregoing restrictions, shall be deemed contrary to law, invalid or unenforceable in any respect by any court or other tribunal of competent jurisdiction, then such restrictions shall be deemed entirely void and unenforceable, and all other restrictions shall be interpreted in light of this unenforceability.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the severability clause with one that voids the entire agreement if any part is unenforceable significantly changes the risk allocation. It could lead to the entire non-compete agreement being invalidated even for minor, easily fixable issues. This may contradict common law principles that favor severing invalid provisions to uphold the remainder of the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184(1) states that if part of a promise is unenforceable on public policy grounds, a court may nevertheless enforce the rest of the agreement if doing so is appropriate considering the agreement's purpose and the extent to which the unenforceability is central to the agreement. The modified clause eliminates this flexibility, potentially leading to a result contrary to the intent of the parties and public policy favoring enforcement of contracts where possible. While parties are free to contract as they wish, a broad clause invalidating the entire agreement goes against the established principles of severability and creates unpredictability.
perturbation.justification: The modification replaces a severability clause, which allows a court to enforce the valid portions of a contract even if some parts are invalid, with a clause that voids the entire agreement if any part is unenforceable. According to the Law Explanation, this eliminates the flexibility that courts have in enforcing the rest of the agreement if doing so is appropriate. This contradiction is significant because it goes against the established principles of severability and creates unpredictability as stated in the Law Explanation, potentially leading to the entire agreement being invalidated even for minor issues.
